title: American Pot Roast
subtitle: Beef chuck braised in red wine and stock until fork-tender, with root vegetables
sourceNotes: Pot roast is a foundational dish of American home cooking, appearing in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and a fixture of American cookbooks throughout the twentieth century. The method of braising a whole cut in the oven is adapted from European braise traditions brought by German, Eastern European, and British immigrants.

content.text: The sear on the beef before

is the flavour base of everything that follows. Sear all four flat sides (bottom, top, and both long sides) until deeply, confidently brown. This takes 16 to 20 minutes total and requires patience and high heat. Pale searing produces a pale sauce.

Heat the oven to 150 °C fan. Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef and dust with flour. Sear all sides in hot oil for 4 to 5 minutes per side. Remove. Cook onion wedges in the remaining fat for 4 to 5 minutes, add garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute. Add {{red-wine}} of red wine and scrape the base, then add {{beef-stock}} of hot stock and Worcestershire sauce. Return the beef, add herbs, cover, and braise for 2 hours.

Add {{carrot}} of carrots and {{waxy-potatoes}} of halved potatoes. Return to the oven covered for 45 to 60 minutes until the beef is fork-tender. Rest covered for 20 minutes. If the sauce is thin, reduce it in a separate pan. Carve or pull the beef and serve with the vegetables and reduced braising liquid, scattered with parsley.
